case_name: Norman T. Crabb and Taelor Campbell-Crabb v. Arun Kumar Durgam, M.D. and Shwe Y. Win, M.D.

summary.brief: Norman T. Crabb and Taelor Campbell-Crabb v. Arun Kumar Durgam, M.D. and Shwe Y. Win, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving neurology.

evidence_index.text: Crabb presented to the emergency room complaining of nausea, vertigo, vomiting, and pain in his abdominal, flank, and kidney areas.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Durgam, the first of Crabb’s attending physicians during his hospital stay, ordered a blood test, and 'a number of CT scans to determine whether [Crabb] had suffered a stroke.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The blood test indicated that Crabb’s white blood cell count was elevated, and one of the CT scans indicated that he had 'diverticulosis coli' or bulges in his colon.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Crabb’s abdominal pain grew more and more severe, and his stomach grew bloated.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Win, who took over after Dr. Durgam’s shift, 'noted that [Crabb] had ‘developed severe abdominal pain’ and that a nasogastric tube had to be inserted to decompress his bloated stomach.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Crabb 'called Dr. Dan Martin, a general surgeon with extensive experience in gastric, liver, pancreas, gallbladder and spleen surgery, and told him about his severe abdominal pain and elevated white blood cell count.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Surgery revealed that Crabb’s colon was perforated and that Crabb’s 'entire abdominal cavity was infected and filled with pus.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Goodman opined that the standard of care for a physician 'caring for a patient with acute sigmoid diverticulitis requires . . . urgent surgical evaluation in order to identify in a timely fashion.'
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Goodman

evidence_index.text: Dr. Goodman opined that had appellees not breached the applicable standard of care, 'the degree of contamination of the peritoneal cavity would have been significantly lower.'
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Goodman

evidence_index.text: We reverse the trial court’s judgment and remand the case for further proceedings.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Nausea, vertigo, vomiting, and abdominal pain
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented to the emergency room with nausea, vertigo, vomiting, and pain in abdominal, flank, and kidney areas.

focused_exam.abdomen: Examination findings not specified, but severe abdominal pain and bloating noted later

requestables.name: Blood Test
requestables.description: Blood test to check white blood cell count

reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation of abdominal symptoms

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scan to check for stroke and abdominal issues

reveal.clinical_significance: Presence of diverticulosis, requires monitoring for complications
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of abdominal pathology

requestables.name: Surgical Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with a general surgeon for abdominal pain

reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates severe abdominal pathology requiring immediate intervention

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

key_events.event: Patient arrives at ED with nausea, vertigo, vomiting, and abdominal pain
Blood test and CT scan ordered
Decision to continue treating as stroke

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with severe abdominal pain and elevated white blood cell count
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in managing the patient's abdominal pain?

options.description: Order repeat CT scans and surgical evaluation
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care for suspected acute abdominal pathology

options.description: Continue treating as stroke without further abdominal investigation

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued treating as stroke without further abdominal investigation

expected_action_court.description: Repeat CT scans and surgical evaluation should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for urgent surgical evaluation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to address severe abdominal symptoms and elevated WBC
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Urgent surgical evaluation and repeat imaging
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed severe abdominal infection requiring surgery
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Loss of sigmoid colon, spleen, and appendix

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to address abdominal symptoms
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented severe outcomes

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to order repeat imaging and surgical evaluation
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely intervention could have reduced severity of infection

factual_timeline.event: Patient arrives at ED with nausea, vertigo, vomiting, and abdominal pain

allegations.allegation: Failure to address abdominal symptoms and elevated WBC
